pattern: Fixing how many of the string's ones fall in each of the three parts turns a splitting problem into counting the two independent ranges of gap positions where each cut point can legally fall.
hints: Instead of trying every pair of cut points, first ask how many '1' characters must land in each of the three pieces for them to be balanced.
If the total count of ones isn't divisible by three there is no way to split evenly; if it's exactly zero, any two of the n-1 internal gaps work as cuts.

Otherwise let k be one third of the ones. The first cut can sit anywhere in the run of zeros between the k-th and (k+1)-th one, and the second cut anywhere between the 2k-th and (2k+1)-th one - multiply the sizes of those two independent ranges, modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: minimum length, all ones split evenly one per part
ones count not divisible by three yields zero ways
single one cannot be shared across three non-empty parts
no ones at all, count grows quadratically with length
ones clustered at one end with slack zeros on both sides
ones spread with multiple zero runs between groups, each range width matters independently

stress.description: long all-zero string exercising the pure-combinatorics branch
long random binary string, ones count forced divisible by three via padding

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def numWays(self, s: str) -> int:
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        n = len(s)
        total_ones = s.count('1')
        if total_ones % 3 != 0:
            return 0
        if total_ones == 0:
            return ((n - 1) * (n - 2) // 2) % MOD
        k = total_ones // 3
        ones_idx = [i for i, c in enumerate(s) if c == '1']
        ways1 = ones_idx[k] - ones_idx[k - 1]
        ways2 = ones_idx[2 * k] - ones_idx[2 * k - 1]
        return (ways1 * ways2) % MOD
